pillar.metaDescription: Compare in-home care types, costs, and how to pay. Get free, unbiased help finding senior home care — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) today.
pillar.quickAnswer: In-home care for seniors lets older adults receive help with daily living, companionship, or skilled nursing at home instead of moving to a facility. The national median cost is about $34 an hour for a home health aide in 2025, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey.
pillar.heroSubhead: From a few hours of help a week to round-the-clock support — understand your options, costs, and how to pay. One call connects you to free, unbiased guidance.
pillar.intro: In-home care helps seniors stay safe and independent at home with support that scales from a few hours weekly to 24-hour coverage. This guide explains the care types, real costs, and ways to pay.

sections.h2: What types of in-home care are available?
sections.body: In-home care falls into three broad categories. Companion care provides social support, supervision, and help with errands or light housekeeping. Personal care (home health aide) adds hands-on help with bathing, dressing, grooming, and mobility. Skilled home health and private duty nursing deliver medical care — wound care, medication management, injections — from licensed nurses or therapists. Many families blend types: a companion a few mornings a week, escalating to personal care or live-in support as needs grow. Choosing the right level depends on whether your loved one needs help with daily living tasks, medical care, or simply company and safety oversight. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to talk through which level fits.

sections.h2: How much does in-home care cost?
sections.body: Costs vary by care level, hours, and location. In 2025, the U.S. median rate runs roughly $30–$35 per hour for a home health aide, per Genworth's Cost of Care Survey. Companion care often costs slightly less; skilled nursing costs more. A common 20-hour-per-week schedule lands near $2,800–$3,000 monthly, while around-the-clock care can exceed $20,000 a month. Live-in arrangements can be more affordable per day than hourly 24-hour shift care. Rates are higher in metro areas and on weekends or holidays. Because pricing differs widely, it pays to compare local agencies before committing.

sections.h2: How do families pay for in-home care?
sections.body: Most in-home care is paid out of pocket, but several programs help. Original Medicare covers skilled, part-time home health only when a doctor certifies you are homebound and need skilled care — it does not cover ongoing companion or custodial care. Medicaid (including waivers) covers personal and home care for those who qualify financially. Long-term care insurance, VA benefits like Aid and Attendance, and life-insurance conversions can also help. Some Medicare Advantage plans add limited in-home support benefits. A licensed advisor can map which of these you may qualify for — call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help.

sections.h2: In-home care vs. assisted living: which is right?
sections.body: In-home care keeps a senior in familiar surroundings with one-on-one attention, and it can cost less than a facility when only part-time help is needed. Assisted living bundles housing, meals, social activities, and 24-hour staff into one monthly fee — often more economical once a senior needs many hours of daily support. The tipping point is usually hours of care: light help favors staying home, while extensive daily needs or isolation may favor a community. Safety, social connection, and budget all factor in. Talk it through with a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor before deciding.

sections.h2: How do I find a trustworthy caregiver or agency?
sections.body: Start by deciding between a licensed agency and an independent caregiver. Agencies handle screening, bonding, insurance, payroll, and backup coverage if your caregiver is sick — independents cost less but put hiring, taxes, and liability on you. Vet any agency for state licensing, background checks, caregiver training, and supervision. Ask about minimum-hour requirements, cancellation policies, and how they match caregivers to clients. Read reviews and request references. Medicare-certified agencies meet federal standards for skilled care. To skip the legwork, call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll help connect you with vetted options in your area.

faqs.q: What is the difference between home care and home health care?
faqs.a: Home care (non-medical) provides help with daily living — bathing, dressing, meals, companionship, and errands. Home health care is medical, delivered by licensed nurses or therapists under a doctor's orders, such as wound care or physical therapy. Many seniors use both at once.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for in-home care for seniors?
faqs.a: Medicare covers skilled, part-time home health care only when a doctor certifies you are homebound and need skilled nursing or therapy. It does not pay for ongoing custodial or companion care — help with bathing, dressing, or meals — when that is the only care needed.

faqs.q: How many hours of in-home care do seniors typically need?
faqs.a: It varies widely. Many families start with 4 to 20 hours a week for help with errands, meals, and safety checks. As needs grow, care can scale to daily visits, overnight coverage, or 24-hour support. A free assessment helps match hours to actual needs.

faqs.q: Is in-home care cheaper than a nursing home?
faqs.a: For part-time help, yes — a few hours weekly costs far less than a facility. But around-the-clock in-home care can exceed nursing-home costs. The break-even point is usually 8 to 12 hours of daily care, where facility pricing often becomes more economical.

faqs.q: Can family members be paid to provide in-home care?
faqs.a: In many states, yes. Medicaid self-directed and consumer-directed programs, plus some VA and long-term care insurance plans, allow qualifying family members to be paid caregivers. Rules vary by state and program. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what may be available where you live.

faqs.q: What are signs a senior needs in-home care?
faqs.a: Common signs include difficulty with bathing or dressing, missed medications, weight loss or spoiled food, falls or unsteadiness, unpaid bills, poor hygiene, social withdrawal, or unsafe driving. Noticing several of these together usually means it's time to consider in-home support.

faqs.q: How quickly can in-home care start?
faqs.a: Many agencies can begin care within 24 to 72 hours after an assessment, and some offer same-day starts for urgent needs like a hospital discharge. Skilled home health requires a doctor's order, which can add a short delay. Calling early speeds the process.

faqs.q: How do I get help choosing in-home care?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, unbiased guidance. A licensed advisor can explain care types, estimate costs, review how Medicare, Medicaid, VA, or insurance may help, and connect you with vetted local agencies — all in one call, at no cost to you.

page.metaDescription: Learn in-home care options, costs, and how to pay for senior care at home. Free, unbiased guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: In-home care for seniors provides help with daily tasks, companionship, or medical care so older adults can stay safely at home. The 2025 national median rate is about $34 an hour for a home health aide, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey.
page.heroSubhead: Understand the care types, costs, and payment options — then get matched with vetted help in your area.
page.intro: In-home care helps seniors live safely and independently at home with support that scales to their needs.

sections.h2: What does in-home care include?
sections.body: In-home care ranges from non-medical support to skilled clinical care. Companion caregivers offer supervision, conversation, errands, and light housekeeping. Personal-care aides add hands-on help with bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, and mobility. Skilled home health brings licensed nurses and therapists for wound care, medication management, and rehab. Most families start small and add hours as needs change. The right mix depends on whether your loved one mainly needs companionship, help with daily living, or medical attention. A free phone assessment with 1-800-MEDIGAP can clarify the right starting level.

sections.h2: How much does in-home care for seniors cost?
sections.body: In 2025, U.S. home care averages roughly $30–$35 per hour, per Genworth's Cost of Care Survey, though rates vary by region and care level. Twenty hours a week runs about $2,800–$3,000 monthly. Costs rise for skilled nursing, overnight shifts, and holidays. Live-in care can cost less per day than hourly 24-hour coverage. Comparing local agencies is the best way to find fair pricing for your situation.

sections.h2: How can families pay for senior home care?
sections.body: Options include private pay, Medicaid and state waivers, long-term care insurance, VA benefits such as Aid and Attendance, and certain Medicare Advantage supplemental benefits. Original Medicare covers only skilled, part-time home health for homebound seniors — not ongoing custodial care. A licensed advisor can review which programs you may qualify for. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help navigating the options.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for in-home care?
faqs.a: Any senior who needs help staying safe at home can use in-home care — there's no age or medical requirement for private-pay non-medical care. Skilled home health through Medicare requires a doctor's order and homebound status. Medicaid and VA programs have their own eligibility rules.

faqs.q: How do I start in-home care for a parent?
faqs.a: Begin with an assessment of your parent's daily needs, then choose between an agency and an independent caregiver. Agencies handle screening, backup, and insurance. Many can start within a few days. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help matching needs to local providers.

faqs.q: Is in-home care covered by insurance?
faqs.a: It depends on the policy. Long-term care insurance often covers home care. Medicare covers only skilled, part-time home health. Medicaid covers personal care for those who qualify. Standard health insurance and Medigap generally do not pay for custodial home care.

faqs.q: What's the difference between in-home care and home health?
faqs.a: In-home care is non-medical help with daily living and companionship. Home health is medical care from licensed nurses or therapists under a doctor's orders. Many seniors receive both — an aide for personal care plus a nurse for clinical needs.

faqs.q: How do I get free help choosing in-home care?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427). A licensed advisor will explain care types, estimate costs, review Medicare, Medicaid, VA, and insurance options, and connect you with vetted agencies near you — at no cost.

page.metaDescription: Compare 24-hour home care vs. live-in care, costs, and how to pay. Free guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) today.
page.quickAnswer: 24-hour home care provides round-the-clock caregiver coverage at home, typically through rotating shifts so someone is always awake and alert. It commonly costs $15,000 to $25,000 or more per month in 2025, depending on location and care level.
page.heroSubhead: Round-the-clock support that lets seniors with high needs stay safely at home — understand the options and costs.
page.intro: 24-hour home care keeps a caregiver present at all times, ideal for seniors needing constant supervision or overnight help.

sections.h2: What is 24-hour home care?
sections.body: 24-hour home care means a caregiver is always present and awake, usually via two or three rotating shifts. Unlike live-in care, where a single caregiver sleeps overnight, 24-hour shift care ensures someone is alert around the clock — important for seniors who wander, fall at night, or need frequent repositioning, toileting, or medical monitoring. It suits advanced dementia, recovery after surgery, or end-of-life support. Because no caregiver works the full day, this model avoids overnight gaps in attention.

sections.h2: How much does 24-hour home care cost?
sections.body: Around-the-clock shift care typically runs $15,000–$25,000+ per month in 2025, based on Genworth's median hourly rates applied to continuous coverage. Live-in care, where the caregiver sleeps and isn't paid for all 24 hours, often costs less. Rates rise in high-cost metro areas and for skilled nursing. Compare both models — and check Medicaid, VA, or long-term care insurance — before committing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to estimate costs for your area.

faqs.q: What's the difference between 24-hour care and live-in care?
faqs.a: 24-hour care uses rotating shifts so a caregiver is always awake and alert. Live-in care has one or two caregivers who stay in the home and sleep overnight, paid for fewer working hours. 24-hour care suits seniors who need constant overnight attention; live-in care fits those who mostly sleep through the night.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover 24-hour home care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not pay for around-the-clock or full-time custodial home care. It covers only intermittent, part-time skilled home health for homebound seniors under a doctor's orders. Medicaid, VA benefits, or long-term care insurance may help cover 24-hour care.

faqs.q: When does a senior need 24-hour home care?
faqs.a: 24-hour care is appropriate when a senior can't be left alone safely — advanced dementia with wandering, frequent falls, serious incontinence needs, recovery from major surgery, or end-of-life care. If overnight supervision is essential, round-the-clock coverage may be necessary.

faqs.q: How many caregivers does 24-hour care require?
faqs.a: Typically two to three caregivers rotate through 8- or 12-hour shifts to provide continuous, alert coverage. Agencies coordinate scheduling and backup if a caregiver is unavailable, ensuring no gaps. This is why shift care costs more than live-in arrangements.

faqs.q: How do I arrange 24-hour home care?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. A licensed advisor can compare 24-hour shift care versus live-in options, estimate costs, review ways to pay, and connect you with vetted agencies that can often start within a few days.

page.metaTitle: Respite Care Near Me for Seniors | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Find short-term respite care near you and learn how to pay. Free help for family caregivers — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Respite care provides short-term relief for family caregivers, with a substitute caregiver stepping in for hours, days, or weeks at home, in an adult day center, or in a facility. Medicare covers respite care for hospice patients up to 5 consecutive days per stay.
page.heroSubhead: Take a break, recharge, and avoid burnout — short-term care for your loved one when you need it.
page.intro: Respite care gives family caregivers a temporary break while a trained caregiver provides safe, reliable support.

sections.h2: What is respite care and how does it work?
sections.body: Respite care is temporary care that relieves a family caregiver. It can happen in the home with a visiting caregiver, at an adult day center, or as a short overnight stay in an assisted living or nursing facility. Respite can be a few hours so you can run errands, a full day, or a week or more for a vacation or recovery. It helps prevent caregiver burnout, which affects a large share of family caregivers. Arrangements can be one-time or recurring, depending on your needs.

sections.h2: How do I pay for respite care?
sections.body: Private pay is common, but help exists. Medicare covers inpatient respite for hospice patients (up to 5 consecutive days per stay, with a small copay). Medicaid waivers in many states fund respite. The VA offers respite for eligible veterans, and the National Family Caregiver Support Program provides grants through local Area Agencies on Aging. Long-term care insurance may also cover it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find respite options and funding near you.

faqs.q: What does respite care cost?
faqs.a: In-home respite is often billed hourly, around $30–$35 an hour in 2025. Facility-based overnight respite typically runs $150–$400+ per day. Costs vary by location and care level. Some programs, grants, and insurance can offset the expense for qualifying families.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover respite care?
faqs.a: Medicare covers respite care only for hospice patients — up to 5 consecutive days of inpatient care per stay, with a small copay. It does not cover respite for seniors who are not enrolled in hospice. Medicaid, VA, and caregiver grants may help others.

faqs.q: How long can respite care last?
faqs.a: Respite can range from a few hours to several weeks. In-home respite is flexible and hourly. Facility respite stays often run a few days to a couple of weeks. Hospice inpatient respite through Medicare is limited to 5 consecutive days per occurrence.

faqs.q: Where can I find respite care near me?
faqs.a: Respite is available through home care agencies, adult day centers, assisted living and nursing facilities, and your local Area Agency on Aging. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) and we'll help you locate trusted respite options and funding in your area.

page.metaDescription: Learn what private duty nursing covers, what it costs, and how to pay. Free guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Private duty nursing provides one-on-one skilled nursing care at home from a licensed RN or LPN, often for hours or shifts at a time. It typically costs $40 to $100+ per hour in 2025 and is usually paid privately or through Medicaid or long-term care insurance.
page.heroSubhead: Skilled, continuous nursing care at home for seniors with complex medical needs.
page.intro: Private duty nursing delivers hands-on, licensed nursing care at home for seniors with ongoing or complex medical needs.

sections.h2: What does private duty nursing provide?
sections.body: Private duty nursing is one-on-one care from a licensed registered nurse (RN) or licensed practical nurse (LPN) in the home. Unlike intermittent home health visits, it provides continuous, hourly, or shift-based nursing for seniors with complex needs — ventilators, feeding tubes, IV therapy, tracheostomy care, complex medication regimens, or chronic conditions requiring monitoring. The nurse focuses on one patient, allowing close, consistent attention. It's often used after hospital discharge or for long-term management of serious illness.

sections.h2: How much does private duty nursing cost and who pays?
sections.body: Private duty nursing typically runs $40–$100+ per hour in 2025, with RN care costing more than LPN care. Because it's continuous skilled care, it's among the costlier home options. Original Medicare generally does not cover ongoing private duty nursing — only intermittent skilled home health. Medicaid (especially for medically complex cases and via waivers), long-term care insurance, and private pay are the main funding sources. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review what may apply to you.

faqs.q: What is the difference between private duty nursing and home health?
faqs.a: Home health provides short, intermittent skilled visits under Medicare for specific needs. Private duty nursing provides continuous, hourly, or shift-based one-on-one nursing for complex, ongoing needs. Private duty is more intensive and usually paid privately, by Medicaid, or by long-term care insurance.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover private duty nursing?
faqs.a: Generally no. Original Medicare covers only intermittent, part-time skilled home health for homebound seniors — not continuous private duty nursing. Medicaid (often through waivers for medically complex patients) and long-term care insurance are more common funding sources.

faqs.q: Who needs private duty nursing?
faqs.a: Seniors with complex medical needs benefit most — those on ventilators, feeding tubes, IV therapy, or with tracheostomies, serious wounds, or unstable chronic conditions. It's also used for intensive post-hospital recovery requiring continuous skilled monitoring at home.

faqs.q: Can an LPN provide private duty nursing?
faqs.a: Yes. Both RNs and LPNs provide private duty nursing, depending on the care required and state scope-of-practice rules. RNs handle higher-acuity tasks and assessments; LPNs deliver many routine skilled tasks at a lower hourly rate, often under RN supervision.

faqs.q: How do I arrange private duty nursing?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can explain what private duty nursing covers, estimate costs, review Medicaid and insurance options, and connect you with licensed agencies that provide skilled in-home nursing in your area.

page.metaDescription: Learn what companion care includes, costs, and how to pay. Free help finding senior companion care — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Companion care for seniors provides non-medical social support, supervision, and help with errands, light housekeeping, and meals. It typically costs $25 to $32 per hour in 2025 and is usually paid out of pocket, since Medicare doesn't cover non-medical care.
page.heroSubhead: Friendly, non-medical support that eases loneliness and keeps seniors safe at home.
page.intro: Companion care offers seniors social connection, supervision, and help with everyday tasks — without medical services.

sections.h2: What does companion care include?
sections.body: Companion care is non-medical support focused on social engagement and daily assistance. Caregivers provide conversation and company, accompany seniors to appointments or outings, prepare meals, do light housekeeping and laundry, help with errands and grocery shopping, offer medication reminders, and provide general supervision and safety oversight. It does not include hands-on personal care like bathing or medical tasks. Companion care is ideal for seniors who are largely independent but lonely, mildly forgetful, or who need a watchful presence to stay safe at home.

sections.h2: How much does companion care cost?
sections.body: Companion care typically runs $25–$32 per hour in 2025, slightly less than personal or skilled care because it's non-medical. Many agencies have a minimum number of hours per visit. Costs rise in high-cost metro areas and for evening, weekend, or holiday hours. Because Medicare doesn't cover non-medical companion care, it's usually private pay, though some Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and long-term care policies help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for local pricing and funding options.

faqs.q: What is the difference between companion care and personal care?
faqs.a: Companion care is non-medical — conversation, errands, meals, light housekeeping, and supervision. Personal care adds hands-on help with bathing, dressing, grooming, toileting, and mobility. Many seniors start with companion care and add personal care as their needs increase.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover companion care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not cover non-medical companion care. It covers only skilled, part-time home health for homebound seniors. Companion care is typically paid out of pocket, though some Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, and long-term care insurance policies may help.

faqs.q: How many hours of companion care do seniors need?
faqs.a: Many families start with a few hours, two or three times a week, for company, meals, and errands. Hours can increase as needs grow. Agencies often set a minimum visit length, commonly two to four hours, so caregiver time is used effectively.

faqs.q: Can companion care help with loneliness?
faqs.a: Yes. Social isolation raises health risks for older adults, and regular companionship is one of the main benefits of companion care. A consistent caregiver provides conversation, shared activities, and outings that improve mood, engagement, and overall well-being.

faqs.q: How do I find companion care near me?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. A licensed advisor can explain what companion care includes, estimate costs, review payment options, and connect you with vetted agencies offering senior companion care in your area.

children.keyword: home health care services near me

page.metaTitle: Home Health Care Services Near Me | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Find Medicare-certified home health services near you and learn what's covered. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Home Health Care Services Near Me
page.quickAnswer: Home health care services provide skilled medical care at home — nursing, physical, occupational, and speech therapy, and aide visits — ordered by a doctor. Medicare fully covers eligible home health for homebound seniors who need intermittent skilled care, with no copay.
page.heroSubhead: Doctor-ordered skilled care delivered at home — find Medicare-certified providers near you.
page.intro: Home health care brings skilled medical services to your door for seniors recovering from illness, surgery, or managing a condition.

sections.h2: What services does home health care include?
sections.body: Home health care includes skilled nursing (wound care, injections, medication management, monitoring), physical, occupational, and speech therapy, medical social services, and part-time home health aide visits for personal care tied to a treatment plan. It's ordered by a doctor and provided by a Medicare-certified agency. Services are intermittent and goal-oriented — for example, helping a senior recover after a hospital stay, surgery, or a new diagnosis. It's medical care, distinct from non-medical home care that helps with daily living and companionship.

sections.h2: What does Medicare cover for home health?
sections.body: Original Medicare covers eligible home health at no cost when a doctor certifies you are homebound and need intermittent skilled nursing or therapy, and care comes from a Medicare-certified agency. Covered services include skilled nursing, therapy, and related aide visits — with no copay and no deductible for the home health benefit. Medicare does not cover 24-hour care, meal delivery, or custodial care alone. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm eligibility and find certified agencies near you.

faqs.q: What qualifies a senior for home health care?
faqs.a: To qualify under Medicare, a doctor must certify the senior is homebound, needs intermittent skilled nursing or therapy, and care is provided by a Medicare-certified agency under a care plan reviewed by the doctor. Both the need and the homebound status must be documented.

faqs.q: How do I find Medicare-certified home health near me?
faqs.a: You can search Medicare.gov's Care Compare tool for certified agencies and quality ratings, ask your doctor or hospital discharge planner, or call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help finding a trusted, certified provider in your area.

faqs.q: Is home health care free with Medicare?
faqs.a: For eligible homebound seniors using a Medicare-certified agency, the home health benefit has no copay or deductible. You may pay 20% for certain durable medical equipment. Non-covered services, like 24-hour or custodial-only care, are not part of this benefit.

faqs.q: How long does home health care last?
faqs.a: Home health is intermittent and tied to a doctor's care plan, recertified roughly every 60 days as long as you remain eligible and show a continued skilled need. It typically continues until recovery goals are met or skilled care is no longer required.

page.metaDescription: Find and vet caregiver agencies near you for senior home care. Free, unbiased help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Caregiver agencies recruit, screen, train, and manage in-home caregivers, handling background checks, insurance, payroll, and backup coverage. Using a licensed agency costs more than hiring independently but reduces your liability and ensures a replacement if a caregiver is unavailable.
page.heroSubhead: Find vetted, licensed agencies that match seniors with trustworthy caregivers near you.
page.intro: Caregiver agencies take the work and risk out of hiring by screening, training, and managing in-home caregivers for you.

sections.h2: What does a caregiver agency do?
sections.body: A caregiver agency handles the full employment relationship so you don't have to. It recruits and background-checks caregivers, verifies credentials, provides training and supervision, carries liability and workers' compensation insurance, manages payroll and taxes, and arranges backup coverage if your regular caregiver is sick or on vacation. The agency matches a caregiver to your loved one's needs and personality, and a care coordinator oversees the plan. This structure offers accountability and continuity that hiring an independent caregiver on your own usually can't match.

sections.h2: How do I choose a trustworthy caregiver agency?
sections.body: Confirm the agency is licensed in your state and ask how it screens and trains caregivers. Verify it runs criminal background checks, carries bonding and insurance, and supervises caregivers. Ask about minimum-hour requirements, how it handles cancellations and caregiver replacements, and whether a care plan is provided. Request references and read online reviews. For skilled care, look for Medicare certification. Comparing two or three agencies helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll connect you with vetted agencies in your area.

faqs.q: Is it better to use an agency or hire a caregiver directly?
faqs.a: Agencies cost more but handle screening, training, insurance, taxes, supervision, and backup coverage, reducing your risk. Hiring directly costs less but makes you the employer — responsible for vetting, payroll taxes, liability, and finding a replacement if your caregiver is unavailable.

faqs.q: How much do caregiver agencies charge?
faqs.a: Agency home care averages about $30–$35 per hour in 2025, varying by region and care level. The rate covers caregiver wages plus screening, insurance, supervision, and backup coverage. Independent caregivers cost less hourly but shift hiring and liability responsibilities to you.

faqs.q: What questions should I ask a caregiver agency?
faqs.a: Ask whether it's state-licensed, how it screens and trains caregivers, whether it carries bonding and insurance, how it handles caregiver replacements and cancellations, what the minimum hours are, and whether a supervisor oversees care. Request references and check reviews.

faqs.q: How do I find vetted caregiver agencies near me?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, unbiased help. A licensed advisor can explain agency versus independent hiring, review costs and payment options, and connect you with screened, reputable caregiver agencies serving your area.

page.metaDescription: Find a qualified home health aide near you and learn what they do and cost. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: A home health aide helps seniors with personal care like bathing, dressing, grooming, mobility, and light tasks at home. The 2025 national median rate is about $34 an hour, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey, though it varies by location.
page.heroSubhead: Hands-on daily help from a trained aide so seniors can stay safe and comfortable at home.
page.intro: A home health aide provides hands-on personal care that helps seniors manage daily living safely at home.

sections.h2: What does a home health aide do?
sections.body: A home health aide (HHA) provides hands-on personal care: help with bathing, showering, dressing, grooming, toileting, incontinence care, transferring, and walking. Aides also assist with meal prep, medication reminders, light housekeeping, and monitoring a senior's condition. When part of a Medicare home health plan, aide visits support a doctor-ordered treatment goal and work alongside nurses or therapists. Aides complete state-required training and competency testing. They bridge the gap between non-medical companion care and skilled nursing, focusing on activities of daily living.

sections.h2: How much does a home health aide cost?
sections.body: The 2025 national median for a home health aide is about $34 per hour, per Genworth's Cost of Care Survey, with wide regional variation. A 20-hour week runs roughly $2,800–$3,000 monthly. Medicare may cover aide visits at no cost when they're part of a qualifying skilled home health plan for a homebound senior. Otherwise, aides are paid privately or through Medicaid, VA benefits, or long-term care insurance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for local rates and coverage help.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a home health aide and a caregiver?
faqs.a: A home health aide completes state-required training to provide hands-on personal care like bathing, dressing, and transfers. A companion caregiver provides non-medical support such as conversation, errands, and light housekeeping. Aides handle physical care; companions focus on supervision and social support.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for a home health aide?
faqs.a: Medicare can cover part-time home health aide visits at no cost when they're part of a qualifying skilled home health plan — meaning the senior is homebound and also needs skilled nursing or therapy. Medicare won't cover an aide for personal care alone without a skilled need.

faqs.q: Are home health aides certified?
faqs.a: Yes. Home health aides complete state-required training and competency evaluations, and those working for Medicare-certified agencies meet federal training standards (typically 75+ hours). Many also hold CNA credentials. Agencies verify certification, run background checks, and provide ongoing supervision.

faqs.q: How do I find a home health aide near me?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can explain what aides do, estimate local costs, check whether Medicare or other programs may cover visits, and connect you with vetted agencies that provide qualified aides in your area.

page.metaDescription: Learn how live-in care works, what it costs, and how it compares to 24-hour care. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Live-in care places a caregiver in the senior's home full-time, providing daytime help and overnight presence, with scheduled sleep and break periods. It often costs less per day than 24-hour shift care because the caregiver isn't paid for all 24 hours.
page.heroSubhead: A dedicated caregiver living in the home — consistent, around-the-day support for seniors with high needs.
page.intro: Live-in care provides a consistent, full-time caregiver in the home for seniors who need extensive daily support.

sections.h2: How does live-in care work?
sections.body: In live-in care, one or two caregivers reside in the senior's home and provide help throughout the day plus overnight presence. The caregiver gets a private place to sleep, scheduled breaks, and uninterrupted sleep time at night — meaning they aren't actively working every hour. This model offers continuity and a strong personal bond, since the same caregiver is consistently present. It suits seniors who need help during the day and reassurance overnight but generally sleep through the night without frequent care. Agencies arrange relief caregivers for the live-in's days off.

sections.h2: Live-in care vs. 24-hour care: cost and fit
sections.body: Live-in care typically costs less per day than 24-hour shift care because the caregiver sleeps and isn't paid for the full 24 hours. It fits seniors who mostly sleep through the night. 24-hour care uses rotating shifts so a caregiver is always awake — better for seniors who wander, need frequent nighttime help, or require constant monitoring. The right choice depends on overnight needs and budget. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare both for your situation.

faqs.q: How much does live-in care cost?
faqs.a: Live-in care often runs $300–$450+ per day in 2025, varying by region and care level. Because the caregiver sleeps and isn't paid for all 24 hours, it usually costs less per day than continuous 24-hour shift care. Skilled needs and metro areas raise the price.

faqs.q: What's the difference between live-in and 24-hour care?
faqs.a: Live-in care has a caregiver who lives in the home and sleeps overnight, paid for working hours, not all 24. 24-hour care rotates caregivers in shifts so someone is always awake. Live-in suits seniors who sleep through the night; 24-hour care suits those needing constant overnight attention.

faqs.q: Does the live-in caregiver get time off?
faqs.a: Yes. Live-in caregivers require uninterrupted sleep, daily breaks, and regular days off, typically covered by a relief caregiver the agency provides. Labor laws govern sleep and break time, which is why this model isn't the same as one caregiver working 24 hours straight.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover live-in care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare does not pay for live-in or full-time custodial care. It covers only intermittent skilled home health for homebound seniors. Live-in care is typically private pay, though Medicaid waivers, VA benefits, or long-term care insurance may help cover it.

faqs.q: How do I set up live-in care?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can explain how live-in care works, compare it to 24-hour care, estimate costs, review ways to pay, and connect you with vetted agencies that provide live-in caregivers in your area.

children.keyword: does medicaid cover home health care

page.metaTitle: Does Medicaid Cover Home Health Care? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Yes — Medicaid covers home health for those who qualify. Learn what's covered and how. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Does Medicaid Cover Home Health Care?
page.quickAnswer: Yes. Medicaid covers home health care for eligible low-income seniors, including skilled nursing, therapy, and home health aide services. Many states also cover personal and custodial care through Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, which Medicare does not cover.
page.heroSubhead: Medicaid is a major source of home care funding — see what it covers and whether you qualify.
page.intro: Medicaid covers home health care for qualifying seniors, often including the custodial care Medicare won't pay for.

sections.h2: What home care does Medicaid cover?
sections.body: Every state's Medicaid program covers mandatory home health services for eligible enrollees — part-time skilled nursing, home health aide visits, and medical supplies. Many states go further through optional personal care benefits and Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, which fund custodial care like help with bathing, dressing, meals, and homemaking so seniors can avoid nursing homes. Some states also allow self-directed care, letting enrollees hire and even pay family caregivers. Unlike Medicare, Medicaid can cover long-term, ongoing personal care — a key reason it funds the largest share of U.S. long-term care.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Medicaid home health care?
sections.body: Eligibility depends on income and assets, which vary by state, plus a functional need for care. Many seniors qualify through aged, blind, and disabled (ABD) pathways or by needing a nursing-home level of care for a waiver. Because waivers often have limited slots and waiting lists, applying early matters. Some seniors qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid ('dual eligibles'). Rules differ significantly by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help understanding eligibility and applying where you live.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover home health aides?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicaid covers home health aide services for eligible enrollees as part of its mandatory home health benefit, and many states cover additional personal care aides through optional benefits or HCBS waivers. Coverage details and hour limits vary by state.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover custodial or personal care at home?
faqs.a: In most states, yes — through optional personal care benefits or HCBS waivers. This covers help with bathing, dressing, meals, and homemaking, which Medicare does not. Availability, hours, and eligibility vary by state, and some waivers have waiting lists.

faqs.q: Can Medicaid pay a family member to provide home care?
faqs.a: In many states, yes. Self-directed or consumer-directed Medicaid programs let eligible enrollees hire caregivers of their choosing, sometimes including adult children or other relatives. Spouses are often excluded. Rules vary by state, so check your local program.

faqs.q: What's the difference between Medicare and Medicaid home care coverage?
faqs.a: Medicare covers only intermittent skilled home health for homebound seniors. Medicaid covers skilled care plus, in most states, ongoing personal and custodial care for those who meet income and functional rules. Medicaid is the main payer for long-term in-home care.

faqs.q: How do I apply for Medicaid home health care?
faqs.a: Apply through your state Medicaid agency or local Area Agency on Aging. Because waivers can have waiting lists, apply early. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help understanding eligibility, gathering documents, and navigating your state's process.

page.metaDescription: Learn how hourly home care works, what it costs, and how to pay. Free help finding flexible senior care — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Hourly home care provides flexible, part-time help by the hour — for tasks like bathing, meals, errands, and companionship — without committing to full-time care. It averages about $30 to $35 per hour in 2025, with most agencies requiring a minimum of 2 to 4 hours per visit.
page.heroSubhead: Pay only for the hours you need — flexible, part-time support for seniors at home.
page.intro: Hourly home care offers flexible, pay-as-you-go support for seniors who need help only part of the time.

sections.h2: How does hourly home care work?
sections.body: Hourly home care lets families schedule a caregiver for specific blocks of time rather than full-time coverage. A caregiver might come a few mornings a week to help with bathing and breakfast, drive to appointments, prepare meals, do light housekeeping, run errands, or provide companionship and safety checks. Most agencies set a minimum visit length — commonly 2 to 4 hours — so caregiver time is used efficiently. It's the most flexible and affordable entry point to home care, ideal for seniors who are mostly independent but need help with certain tasks or times of day.

sections.h2: How much does hourly home care cost?
sections.body: Hourly home care averages $30–$35 per hour nationally in 2025, per Genworth data, with companion care often a bit lower and skilled care higher. Rates rise for evenings, weekends, holidays, and high-cost metro areas. Because you pay only for scheduled hours, weekly cost depends on your plan — 12 hours a week might run about $1,500–$1,800 monthly. Medicare doesn't cover non-medical hourly care, but Medicaid, VA benefits, and long-term care insurance may help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for local pricing.

faqs.q: What is the minimum for hourly home care?
faqs.a: Most agencies require a minimum visit of 2 to 4 hours, since very short shifts aren't practical for caregivers. Minimums vary by agency and region. If you need only brief check-ins, ask agencies about their shortest visit options before signing on.

faqs.q: How much does hourly home care cost per hour?
faqs.a: Hourly home care averages about $30–$35 per hour in 2025, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey. Companion care can be slightly less and skilled care more. Evenings, weekends, holidays, and high-cost metro areas push rates higher.

faqs.q: Is hourly home care covered by Medicare?
faqs.a: Medicare does not cover non-medical hourly home care for help with daily living. It covers only intermittent skilled home health for homebound seniors. Hourly care is usually private pay, though Medicaid, VA benefits, and long-term care insurance may help cover it.

faqs.q: How do I set up hourly home care?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can explain how hourly care works, estimate local costs and minimums, review payment options, and connect you with vetted agencies offering flexible part-time care in your area.

children.keyword: how much does 24-hour home care cost

page.metaTitle: How Much Does 24-Hour Home Care Cost? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: 24-hour home care runs $15K-$25K+ per month in 2025. See cost factors and how to pay. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How Much Does 24-Hour Home Care Cost?
page.quickAnswer: 24-hour home care typically costs $15,000 to $25,000 or more per month in 2025, based on Genworth's median hourly rates applied to continuous shift coverage. Live-in care, where the caregiver sleeps overnight, often costs less — around $9,000 to $14,000 monthly.
page.heroSubhead: Understand what drives the price of round-the-clock care — and the cheaper alternatives.
page.intro: 24-hour home care is among the costliest care options; understanding the price helps you plan and find savings.

sections.h2: What does 24-hour home care cost per month?
sections.body: Continuous 24-hour shift care — where caregivers rotate so someone is always awake — generally runs $15,000–$25,000+ per month in 2025. That reflects roughly $30–$35 per hour applied to all 720+ hours in a month. Live-in care, where a caregiver sleeps overnight and isn't paid for all 24 hours, is usually cheaper, often $9,000–$14,000 monthly. Skilled nursing pushes costs higher, and high-cost metro areas raise rates further. For comparison, this often exceeds the cost of assisted living or even nursing-home care.

sections.h2: What affects the cost and how can I lower it?
sections.body: Cost drivers include your region, the care level (companion vs. personal vs. skilled), shift versus live-in model, weekend and holiday premiums, and agency versus independent hiring. To reduce cost, consider live-in care if your loved one sleeps through the night, mix family caregiving with paid hours, or check funding sources. Medicare won't cover 24-hour care, but Medicaid waivers, VA Aid and Attendance, and long-term care insurance can offset it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a personalized cost estimate and payment review.

faqs.q: Why is 24-hour home care so expensive?
faqs.a: Because it requires paying caregivers for every hour of the day, usually across two or three rotating shifts so someone is always awake. At roughly $30–$35 an hour, continuous coverage adds up to $15,000–$25,000+ monthly. Live-in care, with overnight sleep time, costs less.

faqs.q: Is 24-hour home care cheaper than a nursing home?
faqs.a: Often not. Continuous 24-hour shift care frequently costs more than a nursing home or assisted living. When a senior needs constant care, a facility may be more economical. Live-in care is a lower-cost home alternative if the senior sleeps through the night.

faqs.q: Does insurance pay for 24-hour home care?
faqs.a: Medicare does not cover 24-hour home care. Long-term care insurance, Medicaid waivers, and VA benefits like Aid and Attendance may help eligible seniors. Coverage and limits vary, so it's worth reviewing every program you might qualify for.

faqs.q: How can I lower the cost of 24-hour care?
faqs.a: Consider live-in care instead of rotating shifts if your loved one sleeps through the night, blend family caregiving with paid hours, or apply for Medicaid, VA, or long-term care insurance benefits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help finding savings.

page.metaDescription: Home health aides average about $34/hour in 2025. See rates by region and how to pay. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: The national median home health aide hourly rate is about $34 per hour in 2025, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey. Rates typically range from $25 to $40+ per hour depending on location, care level, and whether you hire through an agency.
page.heroSubhead: See what home health aides charge in 2025 — and what drives the price up or down.
page.intro: The home health aide hourly rate varies by region and care level; knowing the range helps you budget and compare.

sections.h2: What is the average home health aide hourly rate?
sections.body: The 2025 national median home health aide rate is about $34 per hour, per Genworth's Cost of Care Survey, with a common range of $25–$40+ per hour. Agency rates run higher than independent caregivers because they include screening, insurance, supervision, payroll, and backup coverage. Companion care often costs a few dollars less per hour, while skilled nursing costs significantly more. Rates have risen steadily in recent years due to caregiver demand and labor shortages, so confirm current local pricing rather than relying on older figures.

sections.h2: What makes the hourly rate higher or lower?
sections.body: Geography is the biggest factor — coastal and high-cost metro areas often exceed $40 an hour, while parts of the South and Midwest run lower. Other factors include care level (companion vs. personal vs. skilled), agency versus independent hiring, weekend and holiday premiums, shorter visits with minimum-hour requirements, and the local caregiver supply. Long-term contracts or more weekly hours can sometimes lower the effective rate. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for current rates in your area and help comparing agencies.

faqs.q: How much do home health aides charge per hour in 2025?
faqs.a: The national median is about $34 per hour in 2025, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey, with most rates falling between $25 and $40+. Rates depend on location, care level, and whether you hire through an agency or directly.

faqs.q: Why do agency rates cost more than independent aides?
faqs.a: Agency rates include caregiver wages plus the cost of background checks, training, liability and workers' comp insurance, supervision, payroll taxes, and backup coverage. Independent aides charge less hourly, but you take on hiring, taxes, and liability as the employer.

faqs.q: Does the hourly rate change for nights or weekends?
faqs.a: Often yes. Many agencies charge premium rates for evenings, overnights, weekends, and holidays. Short visits may also cost more per hour due to minimum-hour requirements. Always ask an agency for its full rate schedule before scheduling care.

faqs.q: How do I find the home health aide rate near me?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can share current local rates, explain what drives pricing, review whether Medicare, Medicaid, or insurance may help, and connect you with vetted agencies in your area.

children.keyword: how to pay for in-home care

page.metaTitle: How to Pay for In-Home Care | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Explore every way to pay for in-home care: Medicare, Medicaid, VA, insurance & more. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Pay for In-Home Care
page.quickAnswer: In-home care is paid through private funds, Medicaid (including waivers), long-term care insurance, VA benefits, and limited Medicare or Medicare Advantage coverage. Original Medicare pays only for skilled, part-time home health — not ongoing custodial care, which families most often fund through Medicaid or private pay.
page.heroSubhead: A clear breakdown of every funding source for senior home care — and how to combine them.
page.intro: Paying for in-home care usually means combining several sources; knowing your options helps you afford the care you need.

sections.h2: What are the main ways to pay for in-home care?
sections.body: There are six common funding sources. Private pay (savings, income, family contributions) covers most non-medical care. Medicaid and HCBS waivers fund skilled and custodial care for those who qualify financially. Long-term care insurance reimburses home care under policy terms. VA benefits, including Aid and Attendance, help eligible veterans and surviving spouses. Medicare covers only intermittent skilled home health for homebound seniors. Some Medicare Advantage plans add limited in-home support benefits. Many families layer these — for example, an LTC policy plus private pay, or Medicaid plus family caregiving.

sections.h2: Creative ways to help cover in-home care
sections.body: Beyond the main programs, several tools can help. Some life insurance policies allow accelerated death benefits or can be converted to a long-term care benefit. A reverse mortgage can free home equity for care while a senior stays at home. Annuities, HSAs, and certain tax deductions for medical-related care may apply. State non-Medicaid programs and Area Agency on Aging grants offer respite and limited support. PACE programs coordinate care for dually eligible seniors. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help finding every source you may qualify for.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for in-home care?
faqs.a: Only partly. Original Medicare covers intermittent skilled home health — nursing and therapy — for homebound seniors under a doctor's orders. It does not pay for ongoing custodial or companion care. Some Medicare Advantage plans add limited in-home support benefits.

faqs.q: Will Medicaid pay for in-home care?
faqs.a: Yes, for those who qualify financially. Medicaid covers skilled home health in every state and, in most states, ongoing personal and custodial care through HCBS waivers. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term in-home care in the U.S., though some waivers have waiting lists.

faqs.q: Can long-term care insurance pay for home care?
faqs.a: Yes. Most long-term care insurance policies cover in-home care, including personal care and sometimes homemaker services, once you meet the benefit trigger — usually needing help with two or more activities of daily living. Check your policy's daily limits and elimination period.

faqs.q: Do veterans get help paying for in-home care?
faqs.a: Often, yes. The VA Aid and Attendance benefit adds to a pension for eligible wartime veterans and surviving spouses who need help with daily living, and it can fund in-home care. Other VA programs also cover home health for qualifying veterans.

faqs.q: How do I find out what I qualify for?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, unbiased help. A licensed advisor can review Medicare, Medicaid, VA, insurance, and other funding sources, explain eligibility, and help you build a plan to pay for in-home care.

page.metaDescription: Compare in-home care vs assisted living on cost, care, and lifestyle. Free guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: In-home care is usually more affordable when a senior needs part-time help and wants to stay home, while assisted living often costs less once extensive daily care is required. The 2025 median for assisted living is about $5,900 per month, compared with roughly $34 per hour for home care.
page.heroSubhead: A side-by-side look at cost, care, and lifestyle to help you choose the right path.
page.intro: Choosing between in-home care and assisted living comes down to hours of care needed, cost, safety, and social needs.

sections.h2: Cost comparison: in-home care vs assisted living
sections.body: In 2025, assisted living has a national median of about $5,900 per month, per Genworth, covering housing, meals, activities, and 24-hour staff. In-home care averages roughly $34 per hour. The math favors home care at lower hours: 20 hours a week runs about $2,800–$3,000 monthly — less than assisted living. But once a senior needs many hours of daily care, home care can exceed $15,000 monthly, making assisted living far cheaper. The crossover is usually around 8–12 hours of daily care needed.

sections.h2: Which is right for your loved one?
sections.body: In-home care wins on familiarity, one-on-one attention, and independence, and it's cost-effective for part-time needs. Assisted living wins on built-in socialization, meals, activities, on-site staff, and predictable monthly cost for higher-need seniors. Consider the hours of care required, fall and safety risks, social isolation, home accessibility, and budget. A senior who's lonely or unsafe alone may thrive in a community; one who's largely independent often prefers home. There's no universal answer. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help weighing both for your situation.

faqs.q: Is in-home care cheaper than assisted living?
faqs.a: For part-time help, yes — about 20 hours a week of home care costs less than the roughly $5,900 monthly median for assisted living. But once a senior needs extensive daily care, home care can cost much more, making assisted living the cheaper option.

faqs.q: When should I choose assisted living over in-home care?
faqs.a: Consider assisted living when a senior needs many hours of daily help, is socially isolated, faces safety risks living alone, or when round-the-clock home care becomes too costly. The built-in meals, activities, and 24-hour staff often provide better value at higher care levels.

faqs.q: Can a senior get the same care at home as in assisted living?
faqs.a: Often yes, and more personalized — home care offers one-on-one attention and skilled nursing can be brought in. The trade-offs are cost at high hours and less built-in socialization. Assisted living bundles care, meals, and community into one setting and price.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover assisted living or in-home care?
faqs.a: Medicare covers neither custodial assisted living nor ongoing custodial home care. It pays only for intermittent skilled home health for homebound seniors. Medicaid, VA benefits, and long-term care insurance are the main sources for both assisted living and in-home care.

faqs.q: How do I decide between in-home care and assisted living?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, unbiased help. An advisor can compare costs for your loved one's needs, weigh safety and social factors, review payment options, and connect you with vetted home care agencies or assisted living communities.

page.metaTitle: Cost of In-Home Care Per Hour 2025 | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: In-home care averages $30-$35/hour in 2025. See rates by care type and region. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Cost of In-Home Care Per Hour
page.quickAnswer: In-home care costs about $30 to $35 per hour on average in 2025, with a national median near $34 for a home health aide, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey. Companion care often costs slightly less, while skilled nursing costs more.
page.heroSubhead: Hourly rates for every type of home care — and what makes them vary.
page.intro: The hourly cost of in-home care varies by care type and region; here's what to expect in 2025.

sections.h2: What does in-home care cost per hour by type?
sections.body: Hourly rates rise with the level of care. Companion care (non-medical support, errands, supervision) typically runs $25–$32 per hour. Personal care and home health aides, who provide hands-on help with bathing and dressing, average about $34 per hour nationally in 2025, per Genworth. Skilled care from licensed nurses costs much more — $40 to $100+ per hour for private duty nursing. These are medians; your local rate depends on geography, the agency, and timing. Knowing which level your loved one needs is the first step to an accurate estimate.

sections.h2: What factors change the hourly cost?
sections.body: Location is the largest factor — high-cost coastal and metro areas often top $40 an hour, while parts of the South and Midwest cost less. Other factors: agency versus independent hiring (agencies cost more but include insurance, screening, and backup), weekend, overnight, and holiday premiums, minimum-hour visit requirements, and total weekly hours. Caregiver shortages have pushed rates up in recent years, so use current figures. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for the hourly cost in your area and help comparing options.

faqs.q: How much is in-home care per hour in 2025?
faqs.a: In-home care averages about $30–$35 per hour in 2025, with a national median near $34 for a home health aide, according to Genworth's Cost of Care Survey. Companion care often runs a bit less; skilled nursing costs considerably more.

faqs.q: Why does the hourly cost vary so much?
faqs.a: Rates depend on care level, location, agency versus independent hiring, and timing. High-cost metro areas can exceed $40 an hour, while lower-cost regions run less. Nights, weekends, holidays, and minimum-hour visits also push the effective rate higher.

faqs.q: Is companion care cheaper than personal care?
faqs.a: Usually, yes. Companion care is non-medical — conversation, errands, supervision, light housekeeping — and often runs $25–$32 per hour. Personal care adds hands-on help with bathing and dressing and averages about $34 per hour, so it typically costs a few dollars more.

faqs.q: How do I get an accurate hourly quote?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can identify the care level you need, share current local rates, explain minimums and premiums, and connect you with vetted agencies that provide transparent hourly pricing in your area.

children.keyword: cost of in-home care per month

page.metaTitle: Cost of In-Home Care Per Month 2025 | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Monthly in-home care costs range from ~$1,500 part-time to $20K+ full-time. See the breakdown. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Cost of In-Home Care Per Month
page.quickAnswer: In-home care costs roughly $1,500 to $3,000 per month for part-time help (10 to 20 hours a week) and $15,000 to $25,000+ for around-the-clock care in 2025. The figure depends on weekly hours, care level, and your region, based on a median rate near $34 per hour.
page.heroSubhead: Monthly cost estimates for every level of home care — from a few hours a week to 24/7.
page.intro: Monthly in-home care costs scale with hours; here's what part-time, daily, and 24-hour care typically run in 2025.

sections.h2: What does in-home care cost per month by schedule?
sections.body: Monthly cost is hours times rate. At a national median near $34 per hour in 2025: 10 hours a week runs about $1,500 monthly; 20 hours about $2,800–$3,000; 40 hours about $5,800–$6,000; and 8 hours a day (56 hours a week) roughly $8,000. Continuous 24-hour shift care reaches $15,000–$25,000+ monthly, while live-in care (with overnight sleep time) often costs $9,000–$14,000. Companion care lowers these figures slightly; skilled nursing raises them. Your region and agency also shift the totals.

sections.h2: How to plan and reduce monthly costs
sections.body: Start by matching hours to actual needs — many families overestimate at first. Consider companion care for lower-need hours and reserve personal or skilled care for when it's truly needed. Live-in care can cut the cost of overnight coverage versus rotating shifts. Then layer funding: Medicaid waivers, VA Aid and Attendance, and long-term care insurance can substantially lower out-of-pocket cost, and blending family caregiving with paid hours helps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a personalized monthly estimate and a review of every program you may qualify for.

faqs.q: How much does in-home care cost per month?
faqs.a: In 2025, part-time care (10–20 hours a week) runs about $1,500–$3,000 monthly, full daytime care around $5,800–$8,000, and 24-hour care $15,000–$25,000+. Live-in care with overnight sleep time often costs $9,000–$14,000 monthly. Totals depend on hours, care level, and region.

faqs.q: How much is 40 hours a week of home care monthly?
faqs.a: At a national median near $34 per hour in 2025, 40 hours a week runs about $5,800–$6,000 per month. Companion care lowers this somewhat, while skilled nursing raises it. High-cost metro areas and weekend or holiday hours also increase the total.

faqs.q: Is live-in care cheaper per month than 24-hour care?
faqs.a: Usually, yes. Live-in care, where the caregiver sleeps overnight and isn't paid for all 24 hours, often runs $9,000–$14,000 monthly — less than the $15,000–$25,000+ for continuous 24-hour shift care. It suits seniors who sleep through the night.

faqs.q: What programs lower monthly in-home care costs?
faqs.a: Medicaid HCBS waivers, VA Aid and Attendance, and long-term care insurance can substantially reduce out-of-pocket monthly costs for those who qualify. Blending family caregiving with paid hours and using companion care for lower-need times also helps lower the total.

faqs.q: How do I estimate my monthly cost?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can calculate a monthly estimate based on your loved one's needs and local rates, review funding sources that lower the cost, and connect you with vetted agencies.

children.keyword: how to find a caregiver for elderly parent

page.metaTitle: How to Find a Caregiver for Elderly Parent | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to finding a trustworthy caregiver for your elderly parent. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Find a Caregiver for an Elderly Parent
page.quickAnswer: To find a caregiver for an elderly parent, first assess their care needs, then choose between a licensed agency and an independent caregiver, verify background checks and references, and confirm the right personality and skills match. Agencies handle screening, insurance, and backup coverage, reducing your risk.
page.heroSubhead: A clear, step-by-step path to hiring safe, reliable care for the parent you love.
page.intro: Finding the right caregiver for an elderly parent takes a clear plan — from assessing needs to vetting and matching.

sections.h2: Step-by-step: finding the right caregiver
sections.body: Start by assessing your parent's needs: Do they need companionship, hands-on personal care, or skilled medical help? Next, decide between a licensed agency (which screens, insures, supervises, and provides backup) and an independent caregiver (lower cost, but you become the employer). Then vet candidates — confirm background checks, verify training and references, and check state licensing for agencies. Conduct an interview, ideally with your parent present, to assess personality fit. Finally, start with a trial period and a clear care plan. Begin the search early, before a crisis forces a rushed decision.

sections.h2: How to vet a caregiver safely
sections.body: Whether agency or independent, safety vetting is essential. Confirm a criminal background check, verify identity and work eligibility, and check references from prior families. For agencies, ask about bonding, liability and workers' comp insurance, training standards, and supervision. For independents, you may need to run your own checks and handle taxes and liability. Watch for red flags: reluctance to provide references, gaps in history, or pressure to skip paperwork. A trial shift reveals a lot. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and we'll connect you with pre-vetted agencies to simplify this.

faqs.q: Where do I start looking for a caregiver for my parent?
faqs.a: Start with a needs assessment, then choose between a licensed home care agency and an independent caregiver. Agencies, your doctor or hospital discharge planner, and your local Area Agency on Aging are good sources. Calling 1-800-MEDIGAP connects you with vetted agencies quickly.

faqs.q: Should I hire through an agency or independently?
faqs.a: Agencies cost more but handle screening, training, insurance, taxes, supervision, and backup coverage if a caregiver is unavailable. Independent caregivers cost less but make you the employer, responsible for vetting, payroll taxes, liability, and finding replacements. Agencies reduce risk and hassle.

faqs.q: How do I make sure a caregiver is trustworthy?
faqs.a: Verify a criminal background check, confirm references from past families, and check training and, for agencies, state licensing, bonding, and insurance. Interview with your parent present, watch for red flags like missing references, and start with a trial period before committing.

faqs.q: Can I pay a family member to care for my parent?
faqs.a: In many states, yes — through Medicaid self-directed programs, certain VA benefits, or long-term care insurance that allows family caregivers. Spouses are often excluded, and rules vary by state and program. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn what's available where you live.

faqs.q: How do I find a vetted caregiver fast?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help. An advisor can assess your parent's needs, explain agency versus independent hiring, review payment options, and connect you with screened, reputable caregiver agencies in your area — often able to start within days.

children.keyword: signs your parent needs in-home care

page.metaTitle: Signs Your Parent Needs In-Home Care | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: 10 warning signs your elderly parent needs in-home care and what to do next. Free help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Signs Your Parent Needs In-Home Care
page.quickAnswer: Key signs a parent needs in-home care include difficulty bathing or dressing, missed medications, unexplained weight loss, falls or unsteadiness, poor hygiene, unpaid bills, spoiled food, social withdrawal, and unsafe driving. Noticing several of these together usually means it's time to arrange support.
page.heroSubhead: Know the warning signs early — and what to do when you spot them.
page.intro: Recognizing the signs your parent needs in-home care early helps you act before a fall or crisis forces a rushed decision.

sections.h2: What are the warning signs to watch for?
sections.body: Watch for changes in three areas. Physical: difficulty bathing, dressing, or walking; unexplained weight loss; poor hygiene; bruises or signs of falls. Household: piled-up mail, unpaid bills, spoiled food, a messy or unsafe home, or scorched cookware. Cognitive and emotional: forgetfulness, missed medications or appointments, confusion, social withdrawal, mood changes, or getting lost while driving. One small change may be nothing, but several together signal that your parent is struggling to manage alone safely. Visits during holidays often reveal these signs clearly. Trust your instincts if something feels off.

sections.h2: What to do when you notice the signs
sections.body: Start with an honest, respectful conversation — focus on safety and independence, not loss of control. Schedule a medical check-up to rule out treatable causes like infections or medication issues. Consider a professional in-home assessment to gauge needs objectively. Then match support to the signs: companion care for isolation and forgetfulness, personal care for hygiene and mobility, or skilled care for medical needs. Acting early prevents falls and hospitalizations. You don't have to figure it out alone — call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance and help finding the right care.

faqs.q: What are the first signs a parent needs help at home?
faqs.a: Early signs include forgetting medications or appointments, difficulty with bathing or dressing, unexplained weight loss, a messy or unsafe home, unpaid bills, and social withdrawal. Noticing several together — especially after a fall or a holiday visit — usually means it's time to consider in-home care.

faqs.q: How do I know if it's time for in-home care?
faqs.a: It's likely time when daily tasks become unsafe or unmanageable: frequent falls, missed medications, poor hygiene, weight loss, or confusion. If you're worried about leaving your parent alone, that instinct matters. A professional in-home assessment can confirm the level of care needed.

faqs.q: How do I talk to my parent about needing care?
faqs.a: Approach it with respect and focus on safety and staying independent at home, not on what they can't do. Listen to their concerns, involve them in decisions, and start with modest help. Framing care as support that keeps them home often eases resistance.

faqs.q: What kind of care does my parent need?
faqs.a: It depends on the signs. Loneliness or forgetfulness suggests companion care; trouble with bathing, dressing, or mobility points to personal care; medical needs call for skilled home health. A professional assessment helps match the right level. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.

faqs.q: Who can help me arrange care for my parent?
faqs.a: Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, unbiased help. An advisor can review the signs you're seeing, explain care options and costs, check what Medicare, Medicaid, VA, or insurance may cover, and connect you with vetted agencies near you.
